p: This document describes how external systems and users connect to the platform. It is the consumer's-eye view: what each client type sends, what it receives, what authentication flow to use.
The platform serves four principal client classes:

tr.td: QGIS, ArcGIS Pro, ArcGIS Enterprise Portal
OIDC JWT (interactive sign-in) or API key
Other platforms consuming this one's data

p: For users of a web application. The web app:

ol.li: Redirects the user to the identity provider's sign-in flow.
Receives an OIDC ID token from the provider on callback.
Sends the token as Authorization: Bearer <token> on subsequent platform requests.
Refreshes the token before expiry using the provider's refresh-token flow.

p: The platform validates the token against the configured trusted issuers and resolves the user's permissions per 03 Authorisation.
A user with a valid token, or an administrator, creates an API key:

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body: POST /rest/auth/me/apikey (self-service, for the calling user) POST /rest/auth/apikeys (admin, with explicit owner)

p: The response contains the raw key, returned exactly once. Subsequent requests send the key as:
A user-scoped API key inherits the calling user's permissions. A group-scoped API key inherits the group's datasets as viewer. Group-scoped keys are the canonical choice for desktop GIS teams.
API keys are revoked by setting the active flag to false in the DynamoDB api-keys table; revocation takes effect within one minute (the authoriser Lambda's warm-container LRU cache TTL).
For raster layers and (via redirect) vector layers exposed as raster.

ol.li: Layer → Add Layer → Add WM(T)S Layer.
Header value: the raw API key.

p: QGIS sends the X-Api-Key header on every tile request. The CDN caches per-key.
Same as WMTS but the URL is https://<cdn>/wms?service=WMS&request=GetCapabilities.
For vector features as features (not as tiles), with access to attributes.

ol.li: Layer → Add Layer → Add WFS / OGC API Features Layer.
Add the X-Api-Key header as for WMTS.

p: The OGC Features API filters collections by the API key's group datasets and applies row-level security.
h2: ArcGIS Enterprise Portal — vector tile service
p: The platform exposes vector tile layers via an Esri-compatible adapter.

ol.li: The platform supplies VectorTileServer metadata, a Mapbox GL v8 style, and tiles in .pbf form.

p: To pass an API key with ArcGIS requests, configure the portal item's request headers or proxy the requests through a service that adds the header.
ArcGIS Pro consumes WMTS via Add Data → Add Layer From URL with the WMTS GetCapabilities URL. Authentication is configured per the Pro version's documentation.

The platform ships a bundled web application — see 15 Map Client — built on React and MapLibre, with embedded GraphiQL, schema-driven editing, reviewed-editing approvals, live job tracking, and dataset history browsing. It consumes every public surface and is the recommended starting point for users who want a working spatial workspace without writing one. The rest of this document covers other client types — desktop GIS variants, generic web maps, and programmatic clients — that integrate against the same APIs.
For map libraries (MapLibre, Leaflet, OpenLayers) used outside the first-party client:

tr.td: Header via XHR or token in URL where supported

p: Most map libraries support setting custom request headers via a transform or fetch override. For public datasets, no auth is needed.
For scripts and integrations, two patterns:
Use any HTTP client; send X-Api-Key on every request. The platform's REST surfaces follow standard JSON conventions.
A small CLI wrapper can manage API key acquisition and refresh. A reference implementation typically:

ul.li: Stores the user's credentials in a local configuration directory.
Acquires JWTs from the IdP on demand.
Caches the JWT for its lifetime; refreshes on expiry.
Adds Authorization or X-Api-Key to outbound requests transparently.

p: Such a wrapper makes manual operations (GET /rest/auth/me, POST /rest/auth/groups, etc.) low-friction without the user managing tokens directly.
The standard onboarding flow for a new partner team or service:

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body:
sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Admin participant Platform participant Consumer Admin->>Platform: Create platform group
(optional default claims) Admin->>Platform: Link datasets to group alt RLS needed Admin->>Platform: Configure RLS rule(s) on dataset(s) end Admin->>Platform: Create group-scoped API key Platform-->>Admin: Raw API key (once) Admin->>Consumer: Securely deliver key Consumer->>Platform: Requests with X-Api-Key Platform-->>Consumer: Filtered data per group + RLS

p: For interactive users rather than service consumers, replace step 3 with an invitation:
The user is invited via email; on first sign-in, the post-authentication hook converts the invitation into a membership.
h2: List collections the caller may access
Submit a feature update through the editing API
p: The editing flow is typically: create session, request a presigned upload URL, upload the file, finalise. Poll the job for status. See 11 Editing Pipeline.

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body: POST /edit-sessions { "dataset_id": "parcels" } POST /edit-sessions/{id}/uploads (returns presigned URL) PUT <presigned URL> (upload the file) POST /edit-sessions/{id}/finalize (kicks off the pipeline) GET /jobs/{id} (poll for status)

p: For public datasets (public: true in the registry), requests do not need credentials. The CDN serves public content from a single edge cache entry not keyed on credentials:
Anonymous requests succeed; the caller receives only public datasets in catalogue responses.
The platform's external surfaces follow these stability principles:

ul.li: OGC API paths (/features/v1/..., /coverages/v1/...) carry a version in the path. Breaking changes increment the version.
STAC paths follow STAC's own versioning (the STAC API spec carries the version).
Admin paths (/rest/...) are versioned implicitly; breaking changes are infrequent and announced.

p: The query layer's GraphQL schema is introspectable and the schema's evolution follows GraphQL's deprecation conventions.

ul.li: Cold-start latency. In scale-to-zero deployments, the first request after idle may take tens of seconds to a couple of minutes (container services). Subsequent requests are fast.
Cache effects. Tiles cache for seven days per credential at the edge; metadata caches for one hour. Permission changes are visible within five minutes.
No reorderable response. OGC Features returns results in a consistent order (typically by feature ID).
Standards conformance. OGC APIs, STAC, WMTS, WMS, TileJSON, MVT all conform to their respective specifications. Clients that work with other implementations of these standards should work here.
